The F-22's sustained supersonic speed advantage over the F-35 reflects a fundamental design trade-off between air superiority and multi-role versatility that continues to shape military aircraft procurement decisions.

Modern CSAR operations integrate unmanned assets, intelligence networks, and multi-service coordination to achieve rapid personnel recovery despite hostile environments and technological countermeasures.

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation safety from investigating accidents after they occur to preventing incidents through systematic risk identification and mitigation before failures happen.

United flight attendants could soon become the highest-paid in the industry.

United's 40% wage increase sets a new industry baseline that will likely force competitor airlines to raise flight attendant compensation, reshaping labor cost structures across the sector.

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran’s RQ-170 Clone Destroyed in U.S. Strikes, CENTCOM Video Shows

A video shared by CENTCOM shows the destruction of an Iranian clone of the RQ-170 Sentinel, which was reverse-engineered after crashing in Iran 15 years ago. An Iranian clone of the secretive U.S. RQ-170 Sentinel ISR drone was apparently destroyed in an airstrike, an Apr. 2, 2026 video by the U.S....

Iran's decade-long reverse-engineering of captured U.S. drone technology demonstrates the operational vulnerability of losing advanced ISR platforms to adversaries.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Illuminated in Orion

NASA astronaut Christina Koch reads on a tablet in the dimly lit Orion crew capsule in this April 3, 2026, photo. To the right of the image’s center, CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen is seen in profile peering out of one of Orion’s windows. Lights are turned off to avoid glare on...

Orion's interior lighting design balances astronaut comfort with window visibility requirements, demonstrating critical human factors engineering for deep-space missions.

General Aviation Apr 4, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Eastern Iowa Airport's multi-use classification enables it to simultaneously serve flight training operations and commercial air carrier traffic, creating operational complexity that requires careful traffic management protocols.

Why The Airbus A220 Is Becoming More Attractive To Southwest Airlines

A closer look at what the aircraft could mean for Southwest, and why its 737-only operations are now at risk.

Southwest's 737-focused fleet strategy faces economic pressure as the A220's superior fuel efficiency and lower operating costs create a compelling business case for fleet diversification.

General Aviation Apr 4, 2026

How Much Does Pilot Training Cost In The US?

The cost of pilot training extends beyond tuition fees, living expenses, simulator time, and other charges that can catch aspiring pilots off guard.

Hidden costs like accommodation, medical certifications, and checkride fees can inflate total pilot training expenses by 30-50% beyond advertised tuition rates.

Safety Apr 4, 2026

RNO Mustang 9 Departure

Pilots departing from runways 17 left and right at Reno-Tahoe International need to pay special attention to the Mustang 9 Departure. It is extremely important that the procedure on the Mustang 9...

The Mustang 9 Departure from RNO requires heightened pilot attention due to specific procedural complexities that differ from standard departure operations.

Military Apr 4, 2026

Why The C-5M Super Galaxy's 28 Wheels Must Rotate 90 Degrees Just To Retract

The brilliant engineering solution enables a massive payload and efficient flight performance.

The C-5M's 90-degree wheel rotation mechanism represents a critical weight-saving innovation that allows the world's largest cargo aircraft to achieve both maximum payload capacity and fuel efficiency simultaneously.

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran Attained A Fleet Of F-14s Even Though The US Regretted It – Now They’re Destroyed

During the Cold War, Iran was viewed as a crucial stop-block for Soviet southern expansion, the F-14 played a role in this effort.

Iran's decades-long operational use of F-14s demonstrates how geopolitical realignments can leave advanced military aircraft in adversarial hands, creating long-term strategic complications.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Apr 4, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all three domains—airlines, airports, and flights—offers more immediate emissions reductions than waiting for next-generation aircraft technology maturation.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Artemis 2 in good shape cruising towards the moon

A day after lighting its engine to head to the moon, the Artemis 2 Orion spacecraft is performing well with only minor issues.

Minor issues on Artemis 2 suggest NASA's deep-space propulsion systems are maturing, validating the technical foundation for sustained lunar operations beyond this mission.

See SpaceNews →

Regulatory Apr 4, 2026

Trump budget seeks $372 million cut to US Essential Air Service program

US President Donald Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget proposal takes aim at the Essential Air Service program, calling for…...

EAS subsidy cuts could force regional carriers to abandon unprofitable routes to smaller communities, potentially consolidating air service around major hubs and creating geographic access gaps.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

NASA Sets Coverage for Northrop Grumman’s CRS-24 Resupply Launch

NASA, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX are targeting no earlier than 8:49 a.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 8, for the next launch delivering science investigations, supplies, and equipment to the International Space Station. Filled with approximately 11,000 pounds of cargo, the Northrop Grumman Cygnus XL...

Northrop Grumman's Cygnus XL variant expands ISS resupply capacity to 11,000 pounds, demonstrating commercial spaceflight's growing role in government infrastructure support missions.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US A-10 hit during Iran rescue mission, pilot ejects and is rescued

A US Air Force A-10 was hit during combat operations tied to the war with Iran on April…...

Combat damage to tactical aircraft during extended Middle East operations underscores maintenance and pilot recovery capability requirements for sustained military aviation missions.

See AeroTime Hub →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

Michael Graham Named NTSB Vice Chairman

The certified transport pilot and NTSB board member will co-head the agency for a three-year term.

Graham's operational pilot background strengthens the NTSB's technical credibility in investigating accidents and formulating safety recommendations.

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

US and Europe revise GPS interference guidelines as spoofing risks grow

Aviation authorities are warning that instances of GPS interference have spread across a widening range of high-risk airspace,…...

Rising GPS spoofing incidents are forcing aviation authorities to establish unified interference detection standards, directly impacting navigation procedure design and crew training protocols.

Military Apr 3, 2026

Updated: A-10 Thunderbolt II Crashed Near the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Officials Tell NYT

Almost the same time as an F-15E Strike Eagle went down over Iran, the U.S. Air Force lost an A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft near the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. officials told NYT that the jet’s pilot was safely rescued. While search and rescue efforts for at least one of the crew members of an […]...

Two separate U.S. military aircraft losses in the Middle East within hours suggest elevated operational tempo or emerging threats in the region requiring reassessment of force readiness.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Meet NASA’s New Artemis II Science Officers

NASA’s first Artemis II science officers—Kelsey Young, Trevor Graff, and Angela Garcia—are pioneering a new era of mission operations by integrating advanced lunar science directly into Mission Control. Their work will shape how future Artemis crews observe, analyze, and deepen our understanding of...

Embedding science officers directly in Mission Control represents a structural shift in lunar operations that will enable real-time scientific decision-making during Artemis missions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Italy’s Argotec plans to scale Florida satellite facility to meet rising US demand

Italy’s Argotec has officially opened its first U.S. satellite production facility, cementing a foothold near Kennedy Space Center in Florida to join other foreign space firms pursuing growing demand from American programs.

Foreign aerospace manufacturers are strategically establishing U.S. production capacity to capture defense and government satellite contracts, signaling intensified competition in American space industrial base.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US F-15E Shot Down Over Iran, One Crew Member Rescued

The fate of the aircraft’s second crew member is unknown.

Loss of a second-generation fighter in contested airspace highlights vulnerabilities in deep strike operations over advanced air defense systems.

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

FAA report proposes sweeping rewrite of Part 141 flight training rules

A 471-page industry report released through the FAA’s Part 141 modernization docket proposes a sweeping rewrite of how…...

The comprehensive Part 141 rewrite addresses outdated curriculum standards and instructor qualification requirements that have constrained pilot training flexibility for decades.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom

Today marks the 100th anniversary of the birth of Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom, born April 3, 1926, in Mitchell, Indiana. As one of NASA’s first seven astronauts, he became America’s second astronaut to fly in space when he launched aboard the Liberty Bell 7 spacecraft on July 21, 1961, just weeks after...

Grissom's pioneering contributions to early spaceflight established critical protocols for crewed spacecraft design and emergency procedures that remain foundational to modern aerospace safety standards.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 3, 2026

A C-130J awaits its next assignment on the flightline.

The C-130J's continued operational presence underscores the aircraft's enduring relevance in military logistics despite decades of service and emerging modernization initiatives.

Electric Apr 3, 2026

ZeroAvia edges closer to cert as FAA issues electric engine conditions

ZeroAvia has this week celebrated a “major step” towards certification of its ZA600 hydrogen-electric powertrain, after the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) published special conditions to certify the system’s electric engine component. The FAA published 33 special conditions for...

ZeroAvia's FAA special conditions for electric engine certification establish regulatory precedent that will accelerate certification timelines for other hydrogen-electric propulsion developers.

See Runway Girl Network →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

White House again proposes steep NASA budget cuts

For the second consecutive year, the White House is proposing a major budget cut for NASA that would significantly impact the agency’s science programs and the International Space Station.

Sustained NASA budget reductions threaten ISS operations and space infrastructure critical to aerospace supply chain development and commercial space capabilities.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Test Pilots and the Road Back to the Moon

For NASA’s first crewed lunar mission since Apollo, aviation experience is one of the clearest threads linking the astronauts aboard Orion.

NASA's Artemis lunar program is deliberately prioritizing test pilot expertise to manage the novel risks of returning humans to the Moon after a 50-year gap.
